title: Class Clue
html:
He is on the road before the sky is fully light, the bus stop bench already behind him. The road gets wider as the day goes on, a second lane appearing and then a third, the asphalt in better condition than the village roads, the power lines thicker along the shoulders. By late afternoon the buildings on the horizon have resolved into actual buildings — concrete shopfronts and low apartment blocks pushed up against each other — and the road has become a proper city road with lane markers and curbs.

The outskirts are quiet, which is a relief. He had been carrying an image of the city's main streets thick with zombies moving in every direction. What he actually finds is empty roads, scattered abandoned vehicles, and the same absolute absence of living people he has been walking through since the village. If the city's worst is concentrated somewhere, it is not here yet.

"Lucky I didn't live here," he mutters. It is not something he expected to say about a city he traveled to for construction work two dry seasons running.

The sun is getting low behind him when he spots the building. It sits back from the road on its own, no other structure nearby. It is completely white, not faded white or weather-stained white but clean white, the kind that has no business existing in a world running without maintenance for three days. He slows. He checks both directions on the road and finds nothing moving. He heads toward it.

Inside is a single room. White tiles, white walls, no windows. In the center sits a black rectangular surface on a low pedestal, smooth and featureless except for a small raised button at one corner. That is the entire room. Lucas stands in the doorway and looks at it. The building is too clean. The table is too deliberate. He walks up to the button and looks at it for a moment, then presses it.

The surface lights up with a Ting! and a holographic display rises above it, the same format as his system profile but different content.

---

[Class Change Initiated!]

[Error, The Player Haven't Reach the Level Requirement Yet!]

---

"Seriously? A system building?" He looks around the room again as if something might have appeared on the walls. Nothing has. He takes out his phone and photographs the room from the center, then the table, then the display. 'At least I'll have a record.' He presses the button a second time in case the error is temporary. The same message appears. He asks the system what level the requirement is. The system does not answer, consistent with its established practice of ignoring questions that require a useful response.

He is still standing there when a second notification opens beside the first.

---

Achievement Clear:

[Silver] Sixty-Eight to Discover the Class Change Building.

Rewards:

Zombie Resistance Vambrace (0/3) [Rare]

---

A leather vambrace materializes on the tile floor beside the pedestal. He picks it up. It is dark brown with close stitching along the edge and a small embossed mark on the inner forearm that he does not recognize. The tag reads 0/3. He turns this over: one piece of a three-piece set. He wonders what the first person to find one of these buildings received. Full armor, probably. He puts that thought away before it becomes a problem and straps the vambrace onto his left forearm.

With nothing else to do in the room he makes a circuit of it to confirm it is secure, then sits down on the tile floor. The heat is immediate. No windows, no gaps, the air has been sitting undisturbed in here long enough to feel close. He looks at the door. 'Opening it is a bad idea.' He thinks about it a few more times before pulling two shirts out of the pouch and rolling them into a pillow shape.

"Life is genuinely hard," he says to the room, and lies down on the tile. The floor is at least cool against his back. He closes his eyes.

***

January 24, 2029

He wakes at 5 am. The heat has not improved. He sits up, tips a small amount of water from the bottle onto his face, and walks circuits of the room while he waits for the sky outside to lighten, counting steps, adjusting the vambrace strap until it sits right. When the gap under the door shows enough light to see by, he plugs the phone into the power bank for twenty minutes, then packs everything away, picks up the spear, and opens the door.

The morning air outside is cooler and he stands in the doorway to appreciate it for a moment before stepping out.

"I'm sure I'm not the only one still alive," he mutters, looking at the empty road. "So where is everybody?"

A girl's scream comes from between two buildings forty meters to his left — high and sharp, the kind that means something is very close. She comes running out of the gap at full sprint, three zombies behind her, and they are covering ground faster than she is.

Lucas stands at the building door with his spear in his hand. 'Should I help or wait it out.' He runs this for about one second. Then he grabs the bat and moves.

"HELP ME! HELP ME!" She spots him and angles toward him. He waves her past and keeps going toward the zombies.

He throws the spear at the lead zombie — the one closest to where the girl had just been — and the point drives into the right side of its chest, knocking it sideways. It does not drop. He leaves the spear and comes in on the second zombie with the bat, swinging down across the skull with a Bang! The skull gives, but the bat handle splits halfway up the shaft from the force of the hit. He stares at it for one beat. Then he throws what remains of it at the third zombie, which takes the broken shaft across the face and staggers back. He gets to his spear, wrenches it free of the first zombie's chest, and drives it through the third zombie's skull.

The girl screams again behind him, sharper. A fourth zombie has come around the far corner of the building and is almost on her. He throws the spear at it and catches the shoulder, slowing it, then sprints the remaining distance with the dagger already out of his pocket. He reaches the zombie and drives the blade into its throat with both hands and his full weight behind it. The zombie goes down under him with a Squelch! and they both hit the ground, Lucas landing knee-first on the zombie's chest. He pulls the dagger free and gets up.

The system chimes.

---

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Received 5 Coins!]

---

"Only three?" He counts the bodies: skull caved, spear through the head, and the one he just put down. The first zombie — the chest hit — is not among them. He turns and spots it near the alley gap, crawling on its arms with green blood spread wide behind it on the road. The chest wound has taken its legs but not its intent. He walks over to it. The zombie tracks him as he crouches down. He drives the dagger through the side of its skull with a Squelch! and holds it until the arms stop.

---

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Received 5 Coins!]

---

He wipes the blade on the road. Ten coins total now, all from the dagger: five from the zombie under the car yesterday, five from the fourth zombie just now, five from this one. Bat kills and spear kills have produced nothing. He is prepared to call that a confirmed pattern rather than a coincidence. He pockets the dagger and goes to retrieve the spear from the fourth zombie's shoulder.

He hears the girl go quiet behind him. He turns. She is standing about six meters back, watching the spot where he just finished the crawling zombie. She is around his age, maybe a little younger, shorter by several centimeters, dark hair cut to the shoulder, school uniform with the collar still buttoned up. Three days into the apocalypse and the collar is still done up. He notes this without comment.

He walks toward her and holds out his hand.

"You should keep moving," he says. "Standing in the open without watching your angles is a bad habit."

The girl looks at his hand, then at his face, then begins to cry — loud enough that he checks the surrounding gaps and rooflines on reflex. He waits. It gets quieter after a moment, and she gets her feet properly under her.

"I thought I was going to die," she says. Her voice is still unsteady.

"You nearly did." He withdraws his hand gently. She grabs it with both of hers before he gets it back and presses her face into his knuckles, which he has no prepared response for. He gives it a few seconds. The grip loosens.

"Thank you," she says.

"You're welcome." He shakes his hand free and looks down the road, because they are still standing in the open and nothing about this part of the situation has changed.
